name: Abel, George Hinman
description: A mixture of football, sticks and nothing. From the football standpoint he has made the varsity team with yearly regularity. And he has increased his renown as featherweight champion kicker of the pigskin, by assiduously engaging in the studies of parabolic curves and the measurement of applied forces under Doc Hulburt. For other characteristics we must turn to the sticks et al. On sticks he is built, and he is wriggling his way through the university with the splintery indifference of rough pine. With a wiggle in his shoulders and a far-reaching swing of his arms, he may be seen waddling through the corridors like a wooden man on pivots. Nothing is also another characteristic of this specimen, and we find him in the realms of exposition, where he wastes most of the time in asking, "Why is a definition?" and "How is a steam engine?"

name: Armstrong, Alfred Gordon
clubs: Class Numerals and Stor. Executive Com. (F.)
Corresponding Secretary of the Athletic Association (S.)
description: An infant prodigy who has not fulfilled the promise of his youth, and is consequently a failure in all things. If he is asked a question in a class, it dawns upon his bemuddled mind that there is such a thing as a recitation. He stammers, blushes, and finally mumbles incoherently. He needs logic to be handed him with a teaspoon or in a nursing bottle in very small doses, otherwise his smoky mind would rapidly fade away. As a sub-freshman, with a single stock trick he made the lacrosse team at the first jump out of the box. After a few games, however, this trick got stale and Armstrong got staler. His stock was exhausted and Armstrong became a scrub, and ever afterward remained a scrub.

name: Brenton, Benjamin Francis Parlett
clubs: Editor and Assistant Business Manager of Hullabaloo
description: Contrary as the off side of a brick wall, Brenton makes it the prime rule of his existence to agree with no one. This critic lacks but a position on Town Topics to give his ideas fully about everyone he knows and about everything he sees and hears. Indeed, so sore is he on his fellow-man that he is endeavoring, by means of long-winded runs through the wilds of Baltimore county, to exterminate all but those, in his mind, the fittest. For unless a man belongs to the track squad, Polly looks upon him as utterly unworthy of a place in the universe. Nevertheless, he is so winning and affable in his manner that he has won for his closest friends both Mrs. Stewart and Captain Schmeisser, of the Lacrosse Team.

name: Frank Gottlob Breyer
description: See who’s here! An argufyer, a gaudy fop, a blatherskite. Dutch always has a very blunt point to make. Of course, no one sees it. Whereupon he buttonholes his victim, shouts in his face, gets all balled up in trying to express something he doesn't know, and winds up with an "Aw, you know what I mean." Furthermore, he attracts attention by his racy raiment. Joseph's many-colored coat was nothing compared to Breyer's piebald shirts, green socks, and wine-colored hatbands. But the noise of this rainbow riot is often outdone with hellish whoops of Bedlam parentage. Even this, however, has ceased to arouse us, but we shall not remain long in our apathy, for Breyer without attention is like a cardinal virtue without a presidential indorsement.

name: Eveleth Wilson Bridgman
clubs: Class Delegate Athletic Board of Governors (J.)
description: Bridgman came to Hopkins to electioneer. At every election he has been a self-appointed and self-announced candidate of various moods, and always a defeated candidate. After he was defeated for the class Presidency the Vice-Presidency was handed him. He aspired to the chief editorship of the Hullabaloo and was relegated to hack work. Such is the fate of genius. Undaunted by these disappointments, however, he devised a new scheme of managing elections. By his new methods every man was to vote for himself and Bridgman hoped by voting twice to carry the election. Defeat, however, still pursued him and finally drove him to Blaney's, where his unappreciated merits found sympathy in the adventurous career of one with No Mother to Guide Her.

name: Brush, Nathaniel Hawley
description: This young man has been the hero of Hopkins since he entered her portals. Few strenuous events have taken place in which he has not played a valiant part. In his freshman year he won great renown as guard extraordinary for the rescue at the Harford Road affair. During his brief junior year he further increased his renown by his intentional attempts to run the 880. Moreover, he has become quite famously famous by his attendance at the dances. Here Brush has come early and stayed late. Without him we wonder what the Cotillon Club would do. In fact, we consider it a serious defect in the dance picture last year that Brush was so far in the background. To represent a hero by a shirt front displayed an astonishing lack of appreciation.

name: Chesney, Alan Mason
clubs: Class Delegate to Athletic Association (F. J.)
description: With a complete collection of all the exam papers ever issued by the university, and a thorough knowledge of every instructor's soft spot, Chesney has come to Hopkins and has won scholarships. His forte is to pick the questions of an exam. Since they had oxygen at Christmas two years ago, and chlorine last year, he advises you to cram on hydrogen this year. But study is not his only care. That Chesney is not a grind the above string of H's testifies. He has made several of the teams, and one Saturday afternoon delighted the matinee maidens with third place in the half mile. Indeed, so prominent has Chesney become that Dolly and I are going to interview him.

name: Jacob Cohen.
description: Cohen is an inquisitive youngster with a motheaten expression, whose lips are designedly shaped for questioning the why, wherefore, and what of things in general. He flits about the laboratories, prying into the fellows' experiments, and rushes back to his desk, and abridges his own work in order that no one may get ahead of him. His behavior in the classroom is of similar nature. He scribbles a few notes, then cranes around, glances at his neighbor's notes, and hastily scribbles some more notes. If Cohen stays at Hopkins much longer, he will worry away about what other fellows do and what easy markers he has missed. Some are born small, some achieve smallness and some have smallness thrust upon them.

name: Cooke, Charles Wythe
description: Whatever other emotions Cooke may arouse, enthusiasm will always be lacking. No matter how deeply concerned he is, Cooke treats the most unusual events with absolute nonchalance. To become excited is beyond his nature. If you tell him a joke or ask him a question he answers with a disinterested smile, glances askant and says not a word. When he speaks it is usually to force upon you some aged limerick which he now repeats for the nth time. Of such a stock he has quite a repertoire, which in some mysterious way grows without acquiring novelty. That something new and original is required, for he plods monotonously along in the virtuous way of the just and the good.

name: Cromwell, William Andrew
description: Here we have smallness and the usual concomitant, self-anointed badness. This bad, bold boy means well, however, for he keeps everybody around him supplied with the big, black cigars he affects, and shares his books and apparatus with everybody in the laboratory. Moreover, he is harmless and the freshmen in a confidential manner approach him to know if KCl, with a three behind it, makes potassium chlorate and without the three makes potassium chloride. Aside from chemistry the chief occupation of Cromwell is to spend money. He gets much satisfaction in telling how many times a day he rides to school and how many nights a week he goes to the show. Cromwell is typical of the fellow who doesn't care how much money he spends for * * * * * * is * * * *

name: Henry Samuel Frank
description: Frank is a lacrosse player by vocation and a student by avocation. He also has the unique record of being a member of the class without having taken a course with us. While there were upperclassmen, Frank always selected courses which would throw him with the higher classmen, but when we became seniors Frank picked out the courses which would throw him with the juniors and the freshmen. Of his lacrosse record he is very proud and on all occasions takes great pains to tell you what Mr. Clark said of Saturday's game. From Frank's point of view you may flunk your exams, get on the black list, quit in the midst of a race or commit all of the cardinal sins, yet if you know lacrosse you are to be counted among the blessed.

name: William Stuart Gorton
description: To us Gorton is only a name; of the individual we know nothing. We hear the name at roll call and have become accustomed to the automatic "Here, sir," but of the personality our impressions are vague. When asked in his freshman year to attend a class meeting, and at another time to come out for "track," he replied that he came to college to study and not to belong to a class or to enter athletics; a good sentiment, no doubt, but three years spent over the grindstone in burning the midnight oil is apt to dull the sharp and clear-cut resolution of the freshman mind. Not only has he come out for the cross-country runs, but he has even sought renown by heralding his name and face to the public on the pages of our class book.

name: Green, Joseph Elliott
clubs: Member of Y. M. C. A.
description: Mr. Green, who was Caesar? Well, ah, Doctor, oh, that is, ah,--- In this mood Green stammers on until someone blurts out that Caesar was a Gaul and Green echoes the answer only to meet a freezing smile from the instructor. Joe cannot figure out the connection between these alternating hems and ahs, and the examination nightmares that await him at the end of each term. It is either his attempts to solve this problem or his vain endeavors to remember the bleaching powder process that drives him aimlessly through the hallways and places on his face the stamp of dull care. Green's redeeming feature is his perseverance. A student ever, a scholar never.

name: Hecht, Morton Emanuel
description: The bell has rung, roll has been called, the lecture has begun. The door slowly opens and in stalks Hecht with the stateliness of a Prussian bodyguard. Without the slightest embarrassment he leisurely seats himself, carefully selects one of a large bundle of notebooks, opens it mechanically and methodically prepares to complete the advance sheet of the corresponding course he is going to give when he gets his degree. As a correspondence instructor we do not doubt that Hecht will be successful, for if there are volumes in smiles as there are in looks, Hecht will be there with the goods.

name: Frank Glen Holmes
clubs: Editor and Asst. Business Manager of Hullabaloo
description: A disciple of Petronius and an admirer of Omar Khayyam, Pete likes to dabble in the mysteries of being and amuses himself by arguing with the dean about slap-dab generalities. He cuts labs for the "Bridge" and then spends the wee hours of the morning in reading history, seeking justification for himself in the behavior of historical characters. At times Holmes is given to whims, and it is no unusual thing for him to spend weeks in tantalizing out a beard and then, in a single night of righteousness, to repentantly shave. However, this spasmodic righteousness is uncertain, and we fear for Holmes lest some day like Peter Schlemiel he will sell his shadow.

name: Hughes, Herman James
description: Perhaps the personality of Hughes is best observed as leader of the Mandolin Club. In this position his mannerisms are suggestive of the unkempt foreign-named bandmasters of the fish belt summer resorts. At other times Hughes has about him the air of hasty decorum due to a rude awakening. He seems always to be in a state of stupor, from which, when addressed, he arouses himself to assume an attitude of rapt attention. His frankness, however, betrays him, since bluffing is beyond his power, for he candidly replies "No, sir," when reminded of an event that occurred a moment since. Like the proverbial Maine farmer, occasionally "he sets and thinks," but most of the time he "jest sets."

name: Frank Newcomer Hack
description: Rich by chance, dumb by nature, a nickel-pitcher by choice, this chip of the whole block has managed to hang on the rear of '08. With his recently acquired millions, he has gone in for a rich man's pleasures with an avidity that gives a sensation of dubiety, such as when Laura Jean Libbey describes life in high society. To keep pace with the hardened Hack spends entire mornings pitching nickels, but his own true love is the gentle art of automobiling. Whizzing up and down Garden Street, tooting his honk horn, Hack feels that he is fulfilling his mission in life. And as for study—well, no rich man does that.

name: Hutzler, Albert David
description: Hutzler is fitting himself for a barker in a millinery shop. Every time he opens his mouth, half his head is off and the workings of his stomach can be seen. True to his calling, this oracle has already learned to scatter advice indiscriminately. Whenever in want apply to him and you will be supplied by the yard, full width or cut on the bias. However, if you follow this advice you may find yourself straddling a stone jar in vain attempt to keep in SO₃, or sucking up acid through a pipette. And at other times you may persist in rendering a series of short, quick sneezes or in childishly explaining why Fe stands for iron. From Hutzler's advice, all blessings flow, except—when they don't.

name: Caleb Guyer Kelly
description: A nervous, excitable little creature whose mania is baseball. He can give you the particulars of every league in existence, even the one to which the Hopkins team belongs. In season and out, he will inflict upon you the latest revision of the rules or the prospective strength of next year's team. This is characteristic of Kelly, for once he grasps an idea, he will never let it go. Six months ago, he started a course in Geology and he is still talking about the fossiliferous jokes of the Archean age. Several years ago he began French, and now he studies nothing else.

name: Loos, Henry Hofmann
description: A prudish young man who sets you a high example of morality by spending much of his time in the Y. M. C. A., where he may be seen daily, sitting in the front window and ogling everything in petticoats that passes. The ruling passion of Loos' life is to study and to get high marks. He has boned so much on this account, that all fellowship has been driven from him. At rare intervals, perhaps when someone makes a bum pun, this good little boy may actually seem to snicker. After exams, Loos can be found hanging around the post office, pouting about the meanness of the instructor or the unfairness of the questions. Of course, under such circumstances he cannot get high marks, and for this reason the iron has entered his soul.

name: Mackenzie, Donald
motto: "I came to Hopkins to study medicine, and they put me in the Freshman Class."
description: Ever since his entrance, Mackenzie has made it his business to show that he is out of place. His childish capers of asking nonsensical questions, of pitching his books along the corridors, and of stamping outside class-rooms, show conclusively that in a university he is out of his element. As an editor, Mackenzie had monstrous ambitions for the Hullabaloo. The Hullabaloo was to be a book that would be worth reading twenty years hence, a book that would have literary merit, despite the fact that neither he nor any other of the editors ever wrote a Spring Paroxysm or a Dream Ecstasy.

name: Arthur Herman Mann
description: Two shadows which may be made to coincide in all their parts by being placed one upon the other are said to be congruent. Given the shadow of Dr. French and that of the man from Kentucky, to prove that they are congruent. First. Since Mann has been in the University, he has shadowed Dr. French so that no part of his shade has been owing to that of the said Doctor — by observation. Second. Nor has any part of the latter been due to the former — by observation. Third. Therefore the shadows are congruent and have been proved so by their having been placed materially one above the other. Q. E. D.

name: Selwyn William Marcus
description: Marcus is undoubtedly a mistake. Surely this chubby little figure was intended for a Teddy Bear, to be set in the parlor window where in rest and quiet he could lounge in the sunshine. His only claim to being a college man is his receipted tuition bill. He strolls into the University occasionally, enters a classroom now and then, and by some means has himself enrolled as a member of 1908, although he might just as properly be set down as belonging to 1928. Marcus is a tailorism person, who has denied himself the privilege of being poor in order to prove that you can be rich and ignorant at the same time.

name: Mathias, Charles Mccurdy
description: Everyone who was at Hopkins in the fall of 1907 will remember Charlie Mathias. He was the manager of the football team, who went about with a bankrupt expression and a hospital roster. His college bred clothes singled him out as a tramping advertisement without the sandwich board. As a son of Luck, he shone like the nickels he tossed with the recklessness of the Outcast of Poker Flat. Mathias always denied his association with the fair sex, but if we can believe Beatrice Fairfax, perhaps he was not such a misogamist after all.

name: Mertie, John Beaver
motto: "Good Use" gave him a 'K+' for his vivid description of a monk, as all that a monk ought to be.
description: The size and capability of this subject as an athlete, at least as a Hopkins athlete, do not agree. Since he had his dramatic fainting fit in the Trophy Room, in which he resembled a collapsing Colossus, he has been generally excused. John Beaver gnaws only at things he fancies. A term passes with this photo-maniac, having studied nothing except how to keep awake the night before exams. Mertie is inconstant and irresolute of purpose. To him Chemistry was the greatest science on earth until Dr. Swartz pictured what a cracking success he could attain breaking stone.

name: Miller, Daniel
description: Words, words, words, my Lord. Miller has the most prolific excuses ready for all occasions. If one importunes him to buy a class picture, he starts off with a "See, here, I tell you how it is," and travels with the speed of a monometer in a gale. This is Miller's habitual manner, but for an occasion extraordinary, as a recital about his fishing trip last summer or about Kirby Flower's jokes, Dan is absolutely beyond control. The sky may fall, the sea dry up, and the sun turn to a huge snowball, but Miller will jabber on forever. He is daily seen on the third floor among the mummies and relics of Egypt, where he is planning, it is said, an excavating expedition in Marsh Market for locating the first schooner that stuck on the bar at Baltimore.

name: McCauley, Robert Henry
description: McCauley is a student as other men are farmers. He has plowed along without doing anything extraordinary or attracting any unusual amount of attention. He is characterized by a slushy vocabulary and a hearty sense of the ludicrous. To McCauley every sunset is a passionate sunset; for him, the day the class was enrolled was a glorious entry, and he has styled our being hazed as "the strenuous actions of our opponents." We are tempted to remark if our readers will permit the bathos, what has been said of another, that if his adjectives overtake him they will murder him. Also, McCauley is delighted at the simplest jests. If you tell him that Peter Piper was a Pecker, he is tickled immensely, and cachinnates with the fervor of a braying ass.

name: Peck, Peter
description: Peck would undertake to improve on the solar system with as much confidence as he would light one of his annoying, evil-smelling cigars. From his own point of view his abilities are boundless, and his qualities unsurpassed. Moreover, if you take Peter's word for it, he is well acquainted among the big bugs; or, to put it in the vernacular, he has a "big drag wid de swell guys." His latest girl is the daughter of a bank president, and he counted among his friends in his junior year many of the diplomats of Washington. Peck claims that his nose is his fortune, and, judging from the way he is continually petting and rubbing it, one would be justified in this conclusion. Another theory, however, has been offered that it is the seat of his Greek learning as well as of the insipid puns and intricate jokes which Peck insists should be made headliners in the Hullabaloo.

name: Price, Herbert Bryan
description: In Price, the college widow has ensnared another victim. If at a class game or at the dances you see the one, the other is found to be within hailing distance. Like Union, they are one and inseparable. Price in the classroom is a quitter. When quizzed, he blushes, wrinkles his brow, scratches his head, and whispers to the next man for information. When turning in an exam paper, Price cringingly approaches the instructor and pleads for the benefit of the doubt, which doubt we are unable to say, for in fact Price is always sailing on a sea of doubts, and we do not wonder that he is shipwrecked on an exam; indeed, we are surprised if he reaches port at all.

name: Reinhard, Ferdinand Oscar Wolfgang
description: Reinhard is a very good exponent of the theory that brain and brawn are not correlative. His effective use of the battering ram in the attempt to free the class president in the Freshman year has given him the reputation of a young Hercules. On the other hand, the remarkable series of questions, foolish in their simplicity, with which he attempts to make miserable the life of each of his instructors, betrays him to have a deplorable lack of judgment and common sense. Reinhard's familiarity with the German tongue, which is instinctively not acquired with him, makes Uncle Henry's class a convenient slumber hour, and has enabled him to snore away the sixteen conditions he shouldered on entrance.

name: Riddle, Lawrence Melville
description: I am a child of infantile propensities, and it may seem odd that I should come forward with a contribution; but having heard that you are about to publish "The Hullabaloo," I cannot, on so momentary an occasion, refrain from offering my services. I beg leave to submit the following account of my debilities. For two years I was on the Booze Getter, and during the whole time I never drank a drop (of ink, of course), and never had an article rejected. Though it was an act bordering on infanticide when I was dumped from the B. G., nevertheless I still have my friend with me, and I pledge you that ME with an H tacked on will drive the Blackest student into the hysterics of hilarity. A Living Riddle.

name: Ridgely, Julian White
clubs: Class Delegate Athletic Board of Governors (S.)
description: Ridgely's good intentions are like the tides of the sea—continually changing. His good intentions, however, usually are in connection with the athletic course; in his academic course, no such attributes have yet been noted, for he has never been known to answer a question. For the most part, Ridgely does little and says less. Julian Ridgely of H. is carte blanche for an A. B. at Hopkins. We fear that he is vowed to perpetual silence, for the only time he is anyways loquacious is when he finds himself in the Trophy Room with that renowned authority of the turf, Hooley, doping out the next day's runnings.

name: Schwartz, George Henry Charles
description: If men would be written on, they should do something about which their biographers could write. In vain we have been taxing our patience to find some virtue or vice by which Schwartz may be known for all time. Besides the great pleasure he takes in lording it over freshmen and in letting it be known that he is now spending his third year at college, the only other thing about Schwartz which merits even a mediocre record is the development of a laugh that would turn the teeth of a saw-filer on edge. For the most part, Schwartz is living in a fool's paradise, which is no doubt painful, not fatal, which is to be regretted.

name: Reginald Oliver Sellman
description: What is it? Man or beast, caterpillar or mosquito? As a matter of fact, Reginald doesn't seem to be anything in particular, nor is he even known to do anything in particular. He just exists like many others of his type and gives no apology for his existence. In Chemistry lectures, he is alternately animated and dejected. When dejected, he curses under his breath and swears aloud at the number of possible carbon compounds. When animated, he torments Jakie Cohen and smiles audibly at Gilpin's pronunciation. When he is cavorting around the Gym, observers always hold their breath for fear lest in encountering one of the many anti-fat machines Sellman will break in half.

name: Streett, David Corbin
description: Doc has had a crack at track, football and lacrosse. In each he has excelled, according to his own idea, which was to exhibit his hairy limbs to those who care to look, and let them draw their own conclusions as to what he could do if he would only stick to it. The idea of what he "might have been" invests his boyish brow with a halo of athletic prowess, and suggests a tender hot-house plant ripened prematurely, only to decay as fast. As a connoisseur of new brands of tobacco, Streett's reputation is as trustworthy as his recommendations of all latest novels. Any book (text books excluded) which tells the story of a broken heart is bound to receive Streett's unqualified endorsement.

name: Sykes, Philip Louis
description: Sykes has two aims which he has unceasingly prosecuted: one is to disgrace the English idiom, the other is to bootlick. These two aims are more or less concomitant at Hopkins, and Sykes has taken full advantage of the collateral position of his ambitions. As for his actual worth we can best understand him if we take him at his own valuation. With his curly crimps and anemic smile Sykes is a veritable lady killer. From his lips words flow with the exquisite grace of rippling water and with a harmony that captivates the hearer. It is with no want of feeling, however, that we say that Sykes inundated his own stock. As in many other cases, so in this case; self-estimated swans always prove, on examination, to be geese.

name: Sweitzer, Channing Ellsworth
description: Sweitzer is an uncouth product of perplexing attributes. At times he is as modest as a high school maiden and his blushes are as scarlet; at other times he is as brazen as a "co-ed," and his suggestions are impertinent. When once he has a slight knowledge of the elements of a subject, he complacently assumes that he knows it all. He took a year in Biology and now is only prevented from attending the Journal meetings because he is an undergrad. With this accumulation of knowledge, however, Sweitzer becomes close-fisted. He will tell you just enough to arouse your attention and then stop, leaving the impression that he is an impostor. That he is an impostor, Bloomfield proved when he showed Sweitzer to be of the Pennsylvania Dutch despite the deft changes that have been made in his name.

name: Tilghman, Richard Lloyd
description: Sprung from the shades of old pagan mythology, Bacchus still lingers in our midst under the guise of Toby Tilghman. With stubby, pug nose and roseate smile, this modern edition of the classical wine god still enjoys the flowing bowl, the merry jest and the pleasures of idle luxuriousness. Loafing is Tilghman's delight and forms the chief labor of his daily life. With genial good humor he loafs around the basement wearily watching the hours creep by or snoozes between classes in the barber shop. He eats, he sleeps, but gladly would he sacrifice both and all if he could only be permitted to live and do nothing.

name: Ralph Coplestone Williams
description: Williams' accomplishments mostly consist in the development of an artistic temperament, so accounted. Maybe he has—and maybe he hasn't—at least, he has appropriated the artist's mannerisms and wanders around in self-centered loneliness. If his name is not known to fame, it must assuredly not be attributed to the fault of its owner. On every sketch, simple or complicated, the most conspicuous position is devoted to the author's signature. We sometimes wonder whether the sketch or the "R. C. Williams" is the purpose of the drawing. His fame would perhaps be given an additional puff if he could be persuaded to display his glee club ability. For those birds that can't sing and do sing we have the utmost sympathy, but for such creatures that can sing and won't sing we rejoice exceedingly that they don't.

name: Francis Thomas Williams, K. A.
description: How such a retiring personality gets along it is hard to say. If one is fortunate enough to engage Williams in conversation, one may be treated to a patronizing pleasantness which is no doubt a rudimentary development of Williams' journalistic instincts. As a journalist he bids fair to become the equal of "Willie Hoist." His preparation so far has consisted in creeping around in the shadow of an athletic coach, eavesdropping, and then making away to manufacture copy with which he mulcts the daily papers. When athletic news is not forthcoming Williams can excavate any old kind of story, in which capacity he far surpassed Colonel Mann and his famous Town Topics methods.

name: Paul Wegefarth
clubs: Class Delegate Athletic Board of Governors (S.)
description: Paul Wegefarth is a subject unimportant in himself. As a freshman he was indifferent and of inconsequence and did not create the impression amongst his fellow-classmen that his future would be in any way distinguished. But for some unknown reason he was taken up and made president of the junior class. This was a dangerous proceeding, for the sense of pride that elated his breast—and perchance his head—made criticism powerless. He became a mixer and attempted to palm off his deficiencies upon us as manifestations of power. At the senior elections, as a minion executing the dictates of others, he did good work. Past favors received were graciously repaid, and the one chord that vibrated through all—that explains why there was no senior track meet, that excuses a rushed and insipid banquet—this entre nous that pervades all is—scheming.

name: Wright, Joseph Purdon
description: Wright came to college from Law School and with his recently acquired legal air endeavored to instill into the Freshman class an appreciation for parliamentary form. Finding them unable to take care of their own meetings, J. P. Wright, Esq., took upon himself the running of them to suit himself. With a few favorite henchmen and a good bit of scheming, he soon had things his own way and offices were bestowed according to the votes one could pull. Along with his politics, however, he did not forget the fair sex; indeed, he became a lover of the worst sort. Those who have seen him at the dances and joint concert have voiced the opinion that he is making a strong bid for the Woman's College Widower.
